channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

  4. The sun’s violent death could look like this  Popular Science

item.title: 'Milestone': Scientists claim to build synthetic cell, raising concerns in step toward artificial life - Fox News

item.title: The growing number of satellites in orbit could soon make telescopes obsolete. 'For astronomy, this would obviously be catastrophic' - Space
item.description:
1. The growing number of satellites in orbit could soon make telescopes obsolete. 'For astronomy, this would obviously be catastrophic'  Space
  2. "Beyond the limit": one million satellites and mirrors in space pose grave threat to the night sky  ESO.org

  4. 1.7 Million Satellites Will Have ‘Devastating Consequences,’ Study Says  Forbes
  5. Planned 1.7 million satellites 'devastating' for astronomy, study says  Euronews

item.title: Astronomers discover a potentially habitable planet just 25 light-years away. 'This one is exciting' - Space
item.description:
1. Astronomers discover a potentially habitable planet just 25 light-years away. 'This one is exciting'  Space
  2. Nearby ‘Super-Earth’ May Have Conditions For Water  Forbes
  3. Astronomers Find Potentially Habitable Super-Earth Just 25 Light-Years Away  Sci.News
  4. Nearby 'Super Earth' may be a better candidate for life than previously thought  Phys.org
  5. Astronomers Say This Nearby 'Super Earth' Could Be More Habitable Than Expected  NDTV

item.title: A tiny “quasi-moon” called Kamoʻoalewa shadows Earth’s orbit and may be a piece of the actual Moon blasted into space by an ancient impact — and China’s Tianwen-2 spacecraft is now closing in to bring a sample back to Earth. - Space Daily
item.description:
1. A tiny “quasi-moon” called Kamoʻoalewa shadows Earth’s orbit and may be a piece of the actual Moon blasted into space by an ancient impact — and China’s Tianwen-2 spacecraft is now closing in to bring a sample back to Earth.  Space Daily
  2. Could this asteroid be a piece of the Moon? A Chinese spacecraft is about to find out  The Conversation
  3. China’s Tianwen-2 mission has (probably) arrived at a quasi-moon of Earth  The Planetary Society
  4. China's Tianwen-2 Probe Has Likely Reached Earth's "Quasi-moon" Kamo‘oalewa. Now The Fun Begins  IFLScience
  5. China Is About To Land On Earth’s ‘Mini-Moon’ — What To Know  Forbes

item.title: Scientists propose launching a giant 'airbag' into space to protect us from solar superstorms — and experts say it's 'quite feasible' - Live Science

item.title: Fossil jaws hidden inside Cretaceous rocks have revealed that 100 million years ago, some octopuses grew into giant predators — possibly longer than a bus and more fearsome than any octopus alive today. - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Fossil jaws hidden inside Cretaceous rocks have revealed that 100 million years ago, some octopuses grew into giant predators — possibly longer than a bus and more fearsome than any octopus alive today.  Space Daily
  2. Giant school bus–sized octopuses may have once ruled the oceans  Science News Explores

item.title: A rare meteorite found in the Sahara may be a surviving shard of a lost Moon-sized world — one that formed beside the young Sun, followed a path unlike Earth or Mars, and was smashed apart while the planets were still being built. - Space Daily
item.description: A rare meteorite found in the Sahara may be a surviving shard of a lost Moon-sized world — one that formed beside the young Sun, followed a path unlike Earth or Mars, and was smashed apart while the planets were still being built.  Space Daily

item.title: A galaxy confirmed this January formed just 290 million years after the Big Bang, and the part astronomers cannot explain is that it is far too large, far too soon, as if the early universe was building things it was not supposed to be able to build yet. - Space Daily
item.description:
1. A galaxy confirmed this January formed just 290 million years after the Big Bang, and the part astronomers cannot explain is that it is far too large, far too soon, as if the early universe was building things it was not supposed to be able to build yet.  Space Daily
  2. The James Webb Space Telescope peered into one of the universe's oldest galaxy clusters, and scientists can't explain what they saw  Space
  3. The most distant lensing cluster reveals a dark matter surprise  Big Think
  4. James Webb Space Telescope catches 6 galaxies merging into one of the largest galaxies in the universe  Yahoo
  5. The JWST Spies Six Galaxies Becoming One  Universe Today

item.title: Antarctica's Blood Falls Hides a Hidden World That's Never Seen The Sun - Yahoo
item.description:
1. Antarctica's Blood Falls Hides a Hidden World That's Never Seen The Sun  Yahoo

  3. The Mystery of the Bloody Waterfall: Scientists Have Finally Explained the Nature of the Phenomenon  UA.NEWS
  4. Antarctica's Blood Falls: Scientists Uncover a 1.5-Million-Year-Old Isolated Ecosystem, Shedding Light on Potential Ice Changes  CPG Click Oil and Gas

item.title: In November, a spacecraft that has spent eight years falling toward the Sun will finally settle into orbit around Mercury, a planet we have seen up close fewer times than almost any other in the solar system. - Space Daily
item.description:
1. In November, a spacecraft that has spent eight years falling toward the Sun will finally settle into orbit around Mercury, a planet we have seen up close fewer times than almost any other in the solar system.  Space Daily
  2. BepiColombo: The spacecraft that spent 8 years travelling to Mercury is finally arriving in 2026  The Times of India
  3. End of the blue glow: BepiColombo turns off solar electric propulsion for Mercury arrival  European Space Agency
  4. Electric blue thrusters propelling BepiColombo to Mercury  European Space Agency

item.title: Demographers estimate that about 117 billion members of our species have ever been born, which means the roughly 8 billion of us alive today are about 7% of everyone who has ever lived - Space Daily

item.title: In 2024, researchers reported something that should have been impossible: metallic nodules scattered across the deep Pacific seafloor appeared to be producing oxygen in total darkness, creating “dark oxygen” without a single ray of sunlight. - Space Daily
item.description: In 2024, researchers reported something that should have been impossible: metallic nodules scattered across the deep Pacific seafloor appeared to be producing oxygen in total darkness, creating “dark oxygen” without a single ray of sunlight.  Space Daily
